title: The French seam
subtitle: A self-enclosing seam for light wovens that fray
excerpt: The French seam encloses both raw edges inside a second line of stitching, leaving no exposed fabric inside the finished item. The right seam for light cottons, silks, and linens where the fray would show otherwise — and the right seam for any unlined item that will be turned out and seen from both sides.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), § Plain Sewing, pp 12-15, Project Gutenberg etext #25640, for the two-pass construction. Weldon's Practical Needlework vol. 4 (1887), § Seams, Internet Archive, for the seam-allowance ratios used here. Encyclopedia Britannica eleventh edition (1911), 'Needlework' entry, for the historical naming and use.

glossaryTerms.definition: The strip of fabric between the cut edge and the line of stitching. Common allowances are 5 mm, 1 cm, and 1.5 cm depending on the construction. Stated up front for every tutorial so the reader can cut to the correct width.
The printed (or intended-public) face of a fabric. The face that ends up visible on the finished item. The opposite face is the wrong side.

The reverse face of a fabric — the side that ends up hidden inside the finished item. On a printed cotton, the printed face is the right side and the paler reverse is the wrong side. On many solids the difference is slight; mark the right side with a pin if you can't tell.

The finished long edge of a woven fabric — the edge produced by the warp threads turning at the loom rather than being cut. The selvedge is denser and stiffer than the rest of the fabric and is usually trimmed off before cutting pattern pieces.

content.text: A French seam is a seam sewn twice. The first pass joins the two pieces with their wrong sides together, leaving a small narrow seam; the second pass folds the pieces along that first line so the raw edges are enclosed, then stitches again to capture them. The finished seam shows a clean line on the visible face and a neat ridge inside, with no fraying edges exposed anywhere.

The French seam belongs on light wovens that fray easily: cotton lawn, cotton poplin, light linen, silk habotai, anything in the 50-130 gsm range. It is also the right seam for unlined items that will be seen from both sides — a tea-light fabric bag, a baby's cotton bib, a sheer summer top. On heavy fabrics (denim, canvas, drill) the doubled allowance is too bulky and a flat-felled seam is the equivalent.
When to use it
Use a French seam when:
The fabric is light (under about 150 gsm) and likely to fray.
The finished item is unlined and the inside will be visible (turned-out bags, light shirts, drawstring laundry bags, baby items).
You do not have an overlocker or a serger and want a finished seam from a regular domestic machine.

Reach for a different seam when the fabric is heavy (flat-felled seam), when the finished item has a lining or a facing covering the inside (plain seam pressed open works), or when the fabric is a knit (a zigzag or stretch seam fits the elasticity better).
What you need
A sewing machine set to straight stitch, 2.5 mm stitch length. Hand-running stitch at 3-4 mm spacing works if you have no machine — the seam strength is much the same on a light fabric.
Polyester all-purpose thread (Tex 27 / size 50) for cotton and synthetic fabrics; cotton thread for cotton fabrics you want the seam to age with; silk thread for silk.
Iron and pressing cloth. Pressing is part of the seam, not optional.
Pins and dressmaking scissors.
The seam, step by step

The total finished seam allowance is 1 cm. You cut the pieces with a 1.5 cm seam allowance because the construction trims part of it away in the middle. State this once at the top of the pattern and use it consistently.
1. Lay the two pieces
wrong sides
together. This is the unusual instruction — every other seam starts
right sides
together. For a French seam you start backwards on purpose, because the second pass will turn the pieces and bring the right sides where they should be.
2. Pin the seam at 90 degrees to the seam line. Light fabrics shift; pins every 8-10 cm keep them in register.
3. Machine straight-stitch along the seam line at 5 mm from the cut edge (so a 5 mm
seam allowance
). Backstitch at the start and the end.

4. Trim the seam allowance back to 3 mm. This is the move that makes the finished seam neat — if the raw edges are wider than 3 mm they will stick out beyond the second seam line and the encasement will fail. Trim cleanly with sharp dressmaking scissors; do not zigzag or pink.
5. Press the seam to one side. The direction doesn't matter for symmetry but does for the next step — pick a side and stick with it for every seam in the project so the inside is consistent.

6. Fold the pieces along the seam line so they are now right sides together, with the trimmed raw edges on the inside. Press the fold flat — sharp, with steam if the fabric tolerates it. The first seam line should sit exactly on the fold edge.

7. Machine straight-stitch a second seam at 7 mm from the new fold. This second line of stitching captures the trimmed raw edges inside. The finished seam allowance is 1 cm (5 mm + 5 mm of fold + the new 7 mm seam — measured from the original cut edge); the encasement is the difference.
8. Press the finished seam to one side. The seam is heavier than a plain seam (it's three layers — two fabric, one fold of trimmed allowance) and benefits from a steamy press to settle it.
What it looks like when it's right

On the right side, you see one clean line of stitching 1 cm from the seam, with no exposed raw edge anywhere. On the wrong side, you see a flat narrow ridge folded to one side, with the second line of stitching at the base of the ridge and the first line of stitching just visible at the very edge of the fold. No fabric fibres should stick out of the seam at any point — if any do, the trimming step in 4 was too generous.
Common mistakes

Starting with the right sides together. The most common French-seam error, because every other seam begins that way. The result is a seam that looks fine until you turn the project out — and then the raw edges are on the outside. The fix: unpick and start again, wrong sides together first.

Not trimming the first allowance back to 3 mm. Untrimmed, the raw edges poke beyond the second seam line and fray inside the encasement. The seam looks fine when it comes off the machine and unravels over the first wash. The fix: trim every time. 3 mm is the right depth for light fabric; on something a little heavier (medium linen, quilting cotton at 115 gsm) you can leave 4 mm.

Not pressing between the two passes. The seam ends up with a slight bias-pull because the fold is rolled rather than crisp. The second seam line then wobbles along the bias. The fix: press the first seam to one side before the second pass. Press the fold flat at step 6 before the second seam too.

Using French seams on heavy fabric. The doubled allowance reads as a stiff ridge and the encasement is unnecessarily bulky. The fix: pick a flat-felled seam or a regular seam with the allowances zigzagged for fabrics over about 200 gsm.
